data.title: Potbelly Gets A Favorable Acquisition Price From RaceTrac
data.description:
Potbelly (NASDAQ:PBPB) is being acquired by RaceTrac (one of the largest privately held US companies) for $17.12 per share in cash. RaceTrac is known for its gas stations and convenience stores, so this is a move into a different industry for it. The deal price is a healthy 32% premium to Potbelly's closing price before the announcement.

Potbelly has been doing well operationally, delivering low-single-digit company-operated same-store sales growth while efficiently controlling costs. It has also been progressing with its efforts to add franchise shops.

The deal appears to be very likely to go through and represents a healthy premium to my estimate of Potbelly's intrinsic value (which I believe would have been around $11 to $12 after its Q2 2025 earnings). The $17+ price would have likely taken Potbelly a couple of years (or more) to achieve on its own.

Potbelly is trading at a very minor discount to the deal price now. The annualized return from holding Potbelly until the deal payment is probably around 5% to 7% from current levels.

Notes About RaceTrac

RaceTrac is a privately held company that operates around 800 convenience stores and is also involved in fuel distribution and trading. It generated approximately $17 billion in revenue during 2024, which is around double its 2018 revenue.

Potbelly and RaceTrac currently only have a relatively limited geographical overlap. Potbelly is still aiming to reach 2,000 locations, and it believes that RaceTrac's resources will help it achieve that goal. Potbelly has a modest presence in the Southeast currently, but has been attempting to expand there. RaceTrac should be able to help with that, as it has over 400 combined convenience store locations in Florida and Georgia.

Notes On Deal Price

The deal price is $17.12 per share (all cash), which values Potbelly at $566 million. Potbelly has no long-term debt.

The sale price involves a favorable multiple for Potbelly, at approximately 16.4x 2025 adjusted EBITDA (at the midpoint of its updated guidance). It is also likely going to be around 60x Potbelly adjusted diluted EPS for 2025.

The deal is also expected to close in Q4 2025 and involves a 32% premium to Potbelly's closing share price before the deal announcement. It is also a 25% premium to Potbelly's prior 52-week high. There will be a tender offer for Potbelly's shares that will last a minimum of 21 business days. If at least 50% of Potbelly's shares are tendered by the end of that period, payment should happen shortly after (appears to be within three business days of the end of the tender offer period).

Q2 2025 Results

Potbelly delivered another strong quarter in Q2 2025, when it reported +3.2% company-operated same-store sales growth and +3.6% average weekly sales growth (which includes franchised locations) compared to Q2 2024.

This was above the 1.5% to 2.5% guidance range it provided for company-operated same-store sales growth and was also a 2.3% sequential improvement compared to Q1 2025. Potbelly's results in Q1 2025 were negatively affected by winter weather, with an estimated 1.5% impact. Even adjusting for that, there was still some further sequential improvement, though.

Potbelly's +3.2% company-operated same-store sales growth was driven by both an increase in transactions (+1.1%) and average check (+2.1%), with the latter attributable to Potbelly taking a 2.7% price increase.

The strong sales helped Potbelly's adjusted EBITDA reach $9.6 million during Q2 2025, a $1.1 million increase compared to Q2 2024. This was towards the high end of its Q2 2025 guidance range for $8.25 million to $9.75 million in adjusted EBITDA.

Potbelly continued to manage its costs well, with food, beverage, and packaging costs at 26.3% of net sandwich shop revenues. This was up a slight 20 basis points compared to Q1 2025, but down 80 basis points year-over-year

Potbelly repurchased a modest amount of shares during the quarter, repurchasing 113,000 shares for approximately $1 million.

Effect On Future Guidance

The strong results in Q2 2025 led Potbelly to increase its full-year guidance around company-operated same-store sales growth by 0.5% as well as its adjusted EBITDA by $1 million.

About 60% of that guidance increase can be attributable to the Q2 2025 outperformance itself, while the other 40% was due to Potbelly having an improved outlook for the second half of the year.

Potbelly provided guidance for +3.25% to +4.25% company operated same-store growth in Q3 2025, which would be a further sequential improvement compared to the +3.2% it reported in Q2 2025.

Notes On Estimated Value

I had previously estimated Potbelly's value at $10 to $11 per share before its Q2 2025 results.

This would have increased to around $11 to $12 after its strong Q2 2025 results. The $17.12 per share deal price is well above that, and RaceTrac appears to be betting that Potbelly can continue delivering low-single-digit company-operated same-store sales growth for multiple years along with meeting/exceeding its franchise store targets (with RaceTrac's help).

Potbelly investors appear to be getting a very good deal (since I believe it would have taken Potbelly at least a couple of years to improve its intrinsic value to $17+ on its own).

Potbelly is trading at a slight (0.6% to 0.8%) discount to the deal price. I believe the deal is very likely to go through at $17.12 per share.

The 21-business-day minimum tender offer period means that the payment would be in mid-October if the tender offer started immediately and the offer period wasn't extended. The tender offer may start up to 10 business days after the deal was announced, though, so payment could come around the end of October.

The 0.6% to 0.8% potential return may translate into an annualized rate of around 5% to 7%, a bit more than the one-month T-Bill rate.

Conclusion

Potbelly received a favorable acquisition bid from RaceTrac, which was at a 32% premium to its prior closing share price. Potbelly's intrinsic value had been increasing due to its strong recent operational performance, but I think it would have taken Potbelly a couple more years to get to a $17+ valuation on its own.

RaceTrac appears to see a lot of future potential in Potbelly, given that it is paying around 16x 2025 adjusted EBITDA and 60x 2025 adjusted diluted EPS for the company.

I believe that the deal is very likely to go through as is. Holding until the deal payment would result in an annualized rate of return (from current levels) that may be a couple percent higher than short-term T-bills.

data.title: FS KKR Capital: At 81 Cents On The Dollar And 14.4% Dividend Yield Is Not A Buy
data.description:
FS KKR Capital (NYSE:FSK) is trading at a substantial discount to its net asset value ("NAV") per share while paying out a double-digit base dividend yield from a credit portfolio seeing elevated stress. The Philadelphia-based BDC last declared a quarterly base cash dividend of $0.64 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $2.56 per share annualized for a 14.4% dividend yield. FSK also declared a supplemental distribution of $0.06 per share, for an incremental dividend yield of 0.34%. The controversy here, and the catalyst behind the common equity pulling back 18% year-to-date, is that dividend coverage of the base distribution is weak. The BDC generated fiscal 2025 second-quarter net investment income ("NII") of $0.62 per share, down 5 cents sequentially and by a material 19.5% from $0.77 per share a year ago. Hence, FSK is only 97% covering its base dividend yield, and 88.6% covering the aggregate. How? Undistributed earnings spillover. This was in the "$450 million range" at the end of the second quarter, which is around $1.60 per share with FSK having 280 million shares outstanding. I last covered FSK in 2023 with a bullish rating; the BDC's dividend coverage has since slipped to demand a more cautious look.

FSK's portfolio had a fair value of $13.6 billion as of the end of the second quarter. This had a 10.6% weighted average yield on accruing debt investments, a 59% allocation to first lien loans, and was 89.1% floating. Bears would be right to highlight that FSK's first lien exposure is far smaller than comparable BDCs, with peer Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) with a 98.2% first lien exposure and Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) with a 76% first lien exposure. The weighted average yield is seeing pressure, down 20 basis points sequentially and 140 basis points over its year-ago comp on the back of Fed rate cuts through the period.

NAV Changes And Yield Headwinds

FSK reported second-quarter ending NAV per share of $21.93, down $1.44 sequentially on the back of NII that failed to cover the base distribution and supplemental distributions. It means that FSK at $17.84 per share is trading at a roughly 19% discount to NAV, or for around 81 cents on the dollar. It also places FSK's decision to pay out 6 cents per share supplemental out of its undistributed earnings spillover as counter to what should be an objective to maintain and/or grow NAV. The BDC was already trading at a substantial dividend yield before the supplemental dividend was announced, and its inclusion further eroded coverage and ramped up the risk profile of FSK.

FSK is essentially being pulled in two directions, and this balancing act will be made more difficult by pending Fed rate cuts following a CPI print for August that came in at consensus. This pretty much locks in a 25 basis points rate cut at the upcoming Federal Open Market Committee meeting on September 17. Critically, the CME FedWatch Tool currently prices in two more rate cuts of an aggregate 50 basis points to end the year as its most likely scenario based on current economic data. August payroll figures were bad, with the U.S. unemployment rate rising to 4.3%, the highest rate since 2021. The drop in NII year-over-year and pending Fed-driven headwinds to the BDC's weighted average yield will need to be countered by a sustained and significant ramp-up of originations. FSK will need to balance paying out a sustainable income with growing its NAV.

Originations, Credit Quality, And Non-Accruals

FSK investment activity stood at $1.4 billion during the second quarter, down roughly $600 million sequentially but up by 11% over its year-ago comp. Crucially, this was against sales and redemptions of $1.65 billion for net investment activity that was negative at $250 million, albeit with adjusted net investment activity positive following changes to the figure for net sales to Credit Opportunities Partners JV ("COPJV"), an off-balance sheet FSK joint venture. The most alarming metric for the quarter was nonaccruals, which constituted 5.3% of FSK's portfolio on a cost basis and 3% of the portfolio on a fair value basis, up markedly from 3.5% of the portfolio at cost and 2.1% at fair value as of the end of the first quarter. This is far above the average for a BDC of FSK's size.

Conclusion

The lack of NII coverage of the dividend and what still seems like a constrained approach to new investments against pending Fed rate cuts makes it difficult to take the discount to NAV per share as a positive investment signal. FSK's distribution of an uncovered supplemental at the detriment of NAV also did not work to compress its yield but only further ramped up the specter of risk surrounding its common equity. The yield is currently not sustainable without the cannibalization of NAV. Hence, FSK is being rated as a hold.

data.title: Caterpillar: Three Growth Drivers Powering The Bull Case
data.description:
Introduction

Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT) is often perceived as a cyclical construction machinery company, but the investment case is far more nuanced than that. If we look beyond short-term fluctuations, there are three key growth drivers that are providing an appealing mix of stability and long-term upside. Within each of the company’s three main business segments, I want to work out the most important developments that investors should be paying attention to. With that said, let’s start with my investment thesis on the company.

Thesis

As mentioned in the introduction, the thesis rests on three main pillars. Caterpillar’s Construction segment acts as the stability anchor for the company. With a large need for infrastructure investments worldwide, programs like the U.S. Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA) are increasing the demand for heavy machinery and equipment. Beyond fiscal spending, private sector construction activity, generally being more cyclical in nature, will likely pick up materially if the Fed’s rate-cutting path continues soon. Within the Resource Industries business, Caterpillar has exposure to the global energy transition that drives demand for metals like copper, lithium, and nickel. With structurally rising prices for these commodities, mine operators are expected to increase their CAPEX investments to replace and expand aging fleets of machines. Last but not least, perhaps the biggest growth driver for the company is the ongoing boom in data center build-outs by hyperscalers. With AI applications becoming more entrenched in our daily lives at a rapid pace, energy security for data centers is crucial, which positions Caterpillar attractively as a market leader for large backup power systems. Together, this transforms the firm from a purely cyclical industrial company into a quality compounder with multiple secular tailwinds. Let’s now take a closer look at these three pieces to the puzzle that are making Caterpillar such an interesting investment opportunity for long-term investors.

Construction Industries: Stability Through Infrastructure Spending

Within its Construction Industries segment, Caterpillar manufactures and sells machinery for infrastructure and housing construction projects, including excavators, wheel loaders, and dozers. On a full-year 2024 basis, the segment accounted for roughly 35% of total revenues. In Q2 2025, Caterpillar reported decreasing revenues of $6.19 billion (-7% y/y) as well as lower earnings of $1.24 billion (-29% y/y). Margins also dipped by 600 bps to 20.1%. While this seems alarming at first glance, we need to take a closer look beneath the surface to get a better understanding of the future outlook for the business.

Segment sales primarily declined because dealers were actively destocking their inventories. If we take a look at dealer activity over a multi-year time horizon, we can see that in 2023, dealers were increasing their inventories significantly, with 2024 levels staying relatively flat. This explains why inventory levels are falling again at the moment, as dealers are slowly reducing stock. Looking at retail sales to end customers, numbers are showing a different picture. Caterpillar’s own retail sales report for the second quarter of 2025 shows that sales to customers on a global basis in the construction segment have increased by +2% q/q. This indicates that while Caterpillar’s revenues have been declining, underlying demand has been robust, leading to potential dealer restocking in the coming quarters. This is based on the assumption that overall demand will stay elevated. My reason for optimism that this will be the case relies on a few key drivers.

First, on the fiscal side, the IIJA, which was passed in 2021 by the Biden administration, allocates around $1.2 trillion in funding, including $550 billion in new spending for roads, bridges, rails, energy, and water infrastructure. For Caterpillar, this provides multi-year demand stability that is independent of private sector construction activity, which tends to be more cyclical. Although the program officially runs through 2026, many of the projects are only now breaking ground and will continue to run well beyond that date. This effectively creates robust demand dynamics over the coming years, driving the need for a restocking of dealer inventories in the near future. A trend of rising infrastructure spending can also be observed in other regions, including Europe and Asia. For example, Germany announced earlier this year that a €500 billion investment fund will be created to ensure massive improvements to the country’s infrastructure over a 12-year time horizon.

Second, on the monetary side, interest rate cuts could have a positive impact on construction activity. CME’s (CME) FedWatch tool currently implies a virtually 100% chance of a rate cut at the September meeting later this month, as can be seen in the chart below.

Because large construction projects are extremely capital intensive, easing financing conditions will likely act as a boost to overall sector activity. As the rate-cutting cycle is still in its early innings, the effect could be materially stronger over the coming quarters, depending on the Fed’s path.

Last but not least, Caterpillar will directly benefit from the structural tailwind of data center build-outs. As demand for artificial intelligence applications is exploding, investments into data centers are following the trend. McKinsey estimates that capacity could more than quadruple by the end of the decade compared to 2023 levels.

The construction of data centers isn’t as cyclical as other construction activities. As long as AI and cloud computing are growing at a fast pace, Caterpillar can benefit from increasing demand for its machines that is less sensitive to macroeconomic conditions.

Taken together, Construction Industries remains a stabilizing segment for Caterpillar, with cyclical headwinds outweighed by structural demand drivers.

Resource Industries: Cyclical Volatility, Structural Upside

Caterpillar’s Resource Industries segment (roughly 17.5% of total revenues) is well positioned to benefit from the global energy transition that is driving demand for metals such as copper, nickel, or lithium. As prices for these commodities go up, mine operators have an incentive to invest in new equipment, as increasing the output will positively impact their earnings potential. Even though sales in the second quarter declined by -4% y/y, there are reasons for optimism. Global capital expenditures in the mining sector are expected to grow by 8% annually between 2025 and 2027, totaling roughly $400 billion. Leading players in the industry have already announced substantial investments to increase mining capacities, like BHP Group (BHP), which is forecasting to invest over $10 billion annually to get to 1 million tons of copper output per year. The futures price for copper has been in a structural uptrend over the last decade, as can be seen in the chart below. Even though pullbacks are typical, the underlying demand should drive prices higher over the long term.

Because decarbonization efforts require massive amounts of the metal for power grids, electric vehicles, and renewable infrastructure, I expect this development to provide a boost to Caterpillar’s sales potential of large haul trucks and shovels, making copper demand the most important driver for the Resources segment. Lithium and nickel are closely tied to the energy transition as well, as they are essential for EV batteries and energy storage solutions.

Iron ore and steel remain key to construction and infrastructure developments worldwide. As I stated earlier, I expect these industries to grow over the coming years, which will lead to improved demand dynamics for related mining equipment as well.

Lastly, gold can act as a hedge against macro uncertainty for the firm. Even if inflation persists and interest rate cuts are delayed, gold usually is viewed as a safe haven investment. This has been the case for the last few years, with the precious metal’s price going up significantly. If prices stay elevated, miners will continue to keep mining outputs high to benefit from that. Market Research Future projects the global gold mining market to grow at a CAGR of 3.5% until 2034, reaching a value of over $300 billion.

Energy & Transportation: Data Centers Drive The Next Leg Of Growth

Caterpillar is commonly known as a manufacturer of large construction machinery and equipment. What often surprises people is the fact that roughly 40% of the company’s total revenues come from their Energy & Transportation (E&T) segment. Within that, Caterpillar manufactures engines, power systems, and transportation solutions, making it the largest segment in terms of revenue contribution. Besides industrial engines for the manufacturing and energy industries as well as propulsion systems for marine applications, the most interesting part of the segment is the product portfolio centered around diesel and natural gas generator sets for backup and primary power. These are used in hospitals, factories, and utilities, but also data centers, which require massive standby power capacity. Caterpillar is one of the market leaders for large backup power systems that are used in every data center to prevent systems from shutting off in times of power failures. As I already discussed for the Construction segment, data center growth is expected to be really strong over the coming years, making it a strategic growth driver for Caterpillar.

Services: Recurring Revenues, Resilient Profits

Before looking at Caterpillar’s current valuation, I want to make a few comments on the services business, which has quietly become one of its main competitive advantages over peers. The company can offer customers an unprecedented dealer network globally that will not only offer quick and easy access to new machines and equipment but also provide maintenance and repair services as fast as possible. In an industry where time is money and operators cannot afford their machines to be standing still for extended periods of time, Caterpillar is setting itself apart from the competition.

The firm has generated $24 billion in service revenues in 2024, up from $22 billion in 2023 and just $14 billion in 2016. Services now account for roughly 39% of total machinery, energy, and transportation revenues (ME&T), which is up from 36% the year before and 25% in 2016. The company’s goal is to achieve $28 billion in service revenues by next year. The main benefit of having a strong services business is based on the effect it has on top and bottom line stability, smoothing out demand fluctuations. Even when cyclical segments like Construction or Resources are slowing down, demand for service offerings will continue to stay high, as existing fleets need to stay functional. Beyond spare parts and maintenance offerings, the firm also implemented digital solutions for fleet monitoring and analytics. The firm reports that customers who are using these tools are spending up to 33% more on aftermarket services and are more likely to remain long-term customers, making growth here a strategic initiative for the company. As service offerings usually do come with higher margins, a higher share of total revenues will also lead to improved profitability metrics for the whole firm, boosting the bottom line potential.

Valuation

To determine whether Caterpillar is a good investment right now, we have to take a look at the relative valuation versus industry peers.

EV/EBITDA (FWD) EBITDA Margin (TTM)
Caterpillar (CAT) 18.03 22.18%
Komatsu (OTCPK:KMTUY) 8.23 19.78%
Deere & Company (DE) 26.97 19.11%
Cummins (CMI) 10.89 15.46%
Sandvik (OTCPK:SDVKF) 13.04 21.54%
Average 15.43 19.61%

Using an EV/EBITDA relative valuation method, I calculated the average multiple for the peer group of construction and mining machinery manufacturers as well as backup power system providers, which is 15.43. This indicates a relative overvaluation of Caterpillar’s shares at the moment. However, I believe that the slight premium is justified. The company not only offers industry-leading margins but also benefits from its extremely hard-to-replicate dealer network, which has helped Caterpillar build the market share it has today based on the value it provides to customers.

Risks to the Thesis

As Caterpillar recently reported, the company is expecting a tariff hit of $1.5 billion to $1.8 billion for the full year, compared with its previous guidance of up to $1.5 billion. As a manufacturing company, Caterpillar is naturally exposed to rising input costs that could impact margins in a negative way. However, I believe that the demand uptick that I am forecasting will compensate for that negative effect. Another risk is tied to the timing of dealer activity. If end customer demand stalls, dealer restocking could be moved further into the future, leading to continued weakness in revenue growth figures. This would likely come with increasing volatility in the stock’s performance; however, I do not view this as a fundamental issue, as it would likely just be a matter of when, not if, dealer demand reaccelerates. For its resources business, the main risk is related to commodity prices going down. That would act as a cap on mining expansions and reduce the need for fleet overhauls. Again, I would expect this to be temporary, as the underlying trend of the global energy transition is not going to stop. Finally, as with every manufacturing company, technological risks are a constant threat of disruption. While data center backup power systems are based on diesel and natural gas today, a shift to alternative fuels or hydrogen could be possible in the future, creating the need for Caterpillar to invest in R&D to stay on top of this highly attractive growth market.

Conclusion

Caterpillar has quietly transformed itself from an old-economy industrial company to a high-quality compounder with structural growth drivers working for it. While the traditional construction business is still a core focus for the firm, its resources and energy segments are increasingly taking over the reins, creating an intriguing investment case for long-term investors. Being involved in themes like the global energy transition as well as the artificial intelligence boom is certainly a great spot to be in, as Caterpillar plays a crucial role in enabling continued growth in these areas. Even though shares have performed well over the past year, I expect the company to continue offering value to investors over the long term. As a consequence, I initiate coverage on Caterpillar with a Buy rating.
